intro:
The Servant of the LORD ('ebed YHWH) is one of Scripture's great titles of honor. To be God's servant is not slavery in disgrace but the dignity of belonging wholly to the true King and carrying out his will. The image runs from the patriarchs to the cross, where it finds its fullest and most surprising meaning.

In the Old Testament the title marks God's chosen instruments. Abraham is called his servant (Genesis 26:24); Moses is "faithful in all my house" as a servant (Numbers 12:7; Deuteronomy 34:5); David is "my servant" through whom God establishes the throne (2 Samuel 7:5; Psalm 89:20); and the prophets are collectively "his servants" sent to call Israel back (Amos 3:7; Jeremiah 7:25). Beyond individuals, God calls the whole nation his servant, chosen and not cast off (Isaiah 41:8–9; Isaiah 44:21) — yet a servant who is blind and deaf, failing the calling (Isaiah 42:19).

Into that failure Isaiah's Servant Songs introduce a singular Servant who will succeed where Israel could not: the one upon whom God puts his Spirit to bring justice to the nations (Isaiah 42:1–4), a light to the Gentiles (Isaiah 49:6), who gives his back to those who strike (Isaiah 50:6), and who — led like a lamb to the slaughter — is "pierced for our transgressions" and "makes many to be accounted righteous" by bearing their iniquities (Isaiah 53:5; Isaiah 53:11). This is the heart of the image: the Servant saves through obedient, substitutionary suffering.

The shadow falls on Christ. Matthew names Jesus the chosen Servant of Isaiah 42:1 (Matthew 12:18); the Ethiopian official is reading Isaiah 53:7–8 when Philip preaches Jesus from that very text (Acts 8:32–35). Jesus himself declares that he came "not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many" (Mark 10:45), and enacts it by washing his disciples' feet (John 13:14–15). Paul gathers it into the great hymn: though equal with God, he "emptied himself, taking the form of a servant," obedient to death on a cross (Philippians 2:7–8). Where Moses, David, and Israel were servants in shadow, Jesus is the true and faithful Servant who actually bears the sin of many and is therefore exalted as Lord.

In him the title is then given to his people: those redeemed by the Servant become servants of Christ, set free precisely by belonging to him (Romans 1:1; 1 Corinthians 7:22). To serve God, the gospel shows, is the highest freedom — and it is cruciform, shaped by the Servant who first served us.
